coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection and imaging of microRNA in living cells with manganese dioxide nanosheet-active DNAzyme
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yuan
Ma, Chunmeng
Ma, Chen
Xiang, Yueci
Mu, Shuai
Zheng, Zhixiang
Liu, Xiaoyan
Zhang, Haixia
coredata.dc:description: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) play an important role in multiple biological processes and can be used as biomarkers for clinical disease diagnosis, so their detection is of great importance. Here, manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheet acts as carrier to deliver DNAzyme probes into cells through endocytosis, where intracellular glutathione (GSH) reduces the MnO2 nanosheet to manganese ions (Mn2+) and releases the probes. The generated Mn2+ can be further used as an effective cofactor to activate the DNAzyme probe, and cleave the DNA strand into two fragments. Then, the miRNA-155 in the cells can hybridize with the cleaved fragment to cause the fluorescence signal change of the probe. The proposed proportional fluorescent method has been applied to the imaging of miRNA-155 in HeLa cells and HepG2 cells with the estimated detection limit (LOD) as 1.6 × 10−12 M. The new method can provide great help for cancer diagnosis and biological research related to miRNA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets
DNAzyme
miRNA-155
Fluorescence imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of variance for linear model
Imaging of miRNA-155 in living cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21575055 ) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51862029 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Experimental principle of detecting and imaging miRNA-155 in living cells based on DNAzyme-MnO2 nanoprobes.
Characterization of MnO2 nanosheets. (A) TEM image. (B) Ultraviolet absorption spectrum.

Fluorescence spectra under different reaction conditions. A: (a) DNAzyme; (b) DNAzyme + Mn2+; (c) DNAzyme + 1.0 × 10−7 M miRNA-155; (d) DNAzyme + Mn2+ + 1.0 × 10−7 M miRNA-155. B: (a) DNAzyme; (e) DNAzyme + 100 μg· mL-1MnO2; (f) DNAzyme + 100 μg· mL-1MnO2 + 1 mM GSH; (g) DNAzyme + 100 μg· mL-1MnO2 + 1 mM GSH + 1.0 × 10−7 M miRNA-155.

(A) The relationship between concentration of miRNA-155 and fluorescence intensity. (a) 0; (b) 1.0 × 10−11; (c) 5.0 × 10−11; (d) 1.0 × 10−10; (e) 5.0 × 10−10; (f) 1.0 × 10−9; (g) 1.0 × 10−8; (h) 1.0 × 10−7; (i) 1.0 × 10−6; (j) 1.0 × 10−5 M. (B) The linear relationship between logarithm of the concentration of miRNA-155 and the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of the system, error bars are the standard deviation of three repeated measurements, and the following is the same as n = 3.
Residual diagram of linear model. (A) Residual histogram. (B) Normal P–P plot of regression standardized residuals.

Selectivity to different types of DNA strands (A) and metal ions (B) (n = 3). (A) Blank: the reaction system without miRNA-155; the concentration of all RNAs is 1.0 × 10−6 M. (B) The concentration of all ions is 5 mM, the concentration of miRNA-155 is 1.0 × 10−7 M.
(A) Images of HeLa cells incubated with DNAzyme-MnO2 nanoprobes at different times (scale bar = 50 μm). (B) Normalized fluorescence intensity (n = 3).

(A) Images of HepG2 cells and normal cells (human umbilical vein endothelial cells, HUVEC) incubated with DNAzyme-MnO2 nanoprobes for 6 h (scale bar = 50 μm). (B) Normalized fluorescence intensity (n = 3).
Comparison of the other detection methods for miRNA-155.
Compare other cell imaging methods for miRNA-155.

row.entry: Based on AuNPs and DNAzyme amplification strategy
Based on a janus three-dimensional (3D) DNA nanomachine
A DNA machine based on branched catalytic hairpin assembly

HeLa cells
MCF-7 cells
MDA-MB-231 cells
Based on porous 3D DNA hydrogel system
A assembled DNA “nano wheel” based on accelerated localized DNA cascade reaction

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent detection and imaging of microRNA in living cells with manganese dioxide nanosheet-active DNAzyme

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Evidence Science Techniques Research and Application, Gansu Province, Gansu University of Political Science and Law, Lanzhou, 730070, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Evidence Science Techniques Research and Application
Gansu University of Political Science and Law

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Evidence Science Techniques Research and Application, Gansu Province, Gansu University of Political Science and Law, Lanzhou 730070, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) play an important role in multiple biological processes and can be used as biomarkers for clinical disease diagnosis, so their detection is of great importance. Here, manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheet acts as carrier to deliver DNAzyme probes into cells through endocytosis, where intracellular glutathione (GSH) reduces the MnO2 nanosheet to manganese ions (Mn2+) and releases the probes. The generated Mn2+ can be further used as an effective cofactor to activate the DNAzyme probe, and cleave the DNA strand into two fragments. Then, the miRNA-155 in the cells can hybridize with the cleaved fragment to cause the fluorescence signal change of the probe. The proposed proportional fluorescent method has been applied to the imaging of miRNA-155 in HeLa cells and HepG2 cells with the estimated detection limit (LOD) as 1.6 × 10−12 M. The new method can provide great help for cancer diagnosis and biological research related to miRNA.

ce:section.ce:para: MicroRNAs (miRNAs) are a class of non-coding functional RNA molecules that exist in eukaryotic cells and consist of about 18–24 nucleotides [1,2]. Studies have shown that miRNA can achieve gene regulation by directly degrading messenger RNA (mRNA) or indirectly inhibiting protein translation, which has a significant impact on the growth, development and apoptosis of organisms [3]. In recent years, more and more studies have shown that the role of miRNA in tumors involves various aspects, including the generation of tumor cells, angiogenesis, invasion and metastasis of tumor cells [4] making miRNA become an important tumor marker for the diagnosis, treatment and prognosis of early cancers [5]. Therefore, the detection of miRNA is of great significance for the diagnosis of cancer and the study of miRNA at the cellular level.

Traditional methods for detecting miRNA include real-time reverse transcription polymerase chain reaction [6,7], microarray chip [8,9], gene sequencing [10,11], northern blotting [12,13] and fluorescence in situ hybridization technology [14,15]. Some of these methods are limited due to the complex operation and many steps. In recent years, new methods for detecting miRNA have been developed, such as electrochemistry [16–18], fluorescence [19–21], Raman spectroscopy [22,23], mass spectrometry [24] and DNA amplification technology [25,26]. These methods can detect low-abundance miRNAs with good sensitivity and wide detection range. However, some of these methods require complicated and expensive instruments or be limited to in vitro detection. Further detection and imaging of endogenous miRNAs in the cells is still a challenge.

Deoxyribozyme (DNAzyme) is an enzyme-active single-stranded DNA fragment derived from systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment (SELEX) technology [27], which can mimic the function of enzymes in vivo [28] and its activity is closely correlated with some metal ions [29]. When specific metal ions are present, DNAzyme can cleave the probe at a specific position to obtain smaller single-stranded DNA fragments. Currently, the most widely used is RNA-cleaving DNAzyme. DNAzyme is widely used in the field of biological analysis due to its advantages of easy synthesis modification, high catalytic activity and strong specificity [30,31]. Therefore, using the characteristics of DNAzyme, we designed a ratio fluorescent probe based on DNAzyme to detect miRNA-155. In order to achieve the purpose of imaging miRNA-155 in cells, we must develop a method to efficiently deliver nucleic acid probes into living cells.

Nanomaterials have become good carriers for the delivery of nucleic acid probes because of their low cytotoxicity and excellent biocompatibility [32–34]. In addition, there are also methods that use quantum dots [35,36], gold nanorods [37] and silicon nanoparticles [38,39] as carriers to achieve intracellular miRNA imaging. These nanomaterials loaded with nucleic acid probes into cells can not only achieve the purpose of detecting and imaging miRNA in living cells, but also monitor the expression level of miRNA in cells. However, most methods require nucleic acid probes to fix on the surface of nanomaterials by assembly or covalently cross-linking. After being internalized into cells, probes cannot be separated from nanomaterials, and most materials cannot be degraded in cells, which limits their further application in living cells. Furthermore, the application and development of DNAzyme in cell imaging is limited by its weak cellular penetration and enzymatic activity that requires a large number of metal ions to activate. Therefore, based on the above problems, it is crucial to develop a carrier that can not only enhance the efficiency of DNAzyme entering cells, but also generate a large number of cofactors to enhance the efficiency of DNAzyme cleavage.

Manganese dioxide (MnO2) nanosheets have attracted wide attention because of their excellent physical and chemical properties [40]. Due to the non-covalent π-π stacking effect of MnO2 nanosheets, they can be used as carriers for nucleic acid probes [41,42]. Furthermore, the wide optical absorption spectrum of MnO2 nanosheets (λ = 300–600 nm) makes it strong fluorescence quenching ability to reduce the background signal of the experiment [43,44]. Besides, MnO2 nanosheets can be reduced by intracellular glutathione (GSH) to generate manganese ions (Mn2+) [45–47], which is an effective cofactor for activating the activity of DNAzyme [48].

Therefore, MnO2 nanosheets have great advantages in delivering DNAzyme probes and improving the imaging of miRNA-155 in living cells. Based above, we developed a novel proportional fluorescent method for imaging of miRNA-155 in living cells, which eliminates the interference of measurement conditions and provides high imaging contrast.
All the informations of chemicals, materials and instruments are in the supporting information.
First, 10 μL of 1.0 × 10−6 M DNA3 reacted with 10 μL of 8.0 × 10−6 M DNA4 at 95 °C for 5 min, then cooled to room temperature to obtain the hybridized DNA probes (DNA3-DNA4, namely DNAzyme).
Then, 100 μL of 100 μg· mL-1 MnO2 nanosheets and 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 7.4) were added. The mixture was fixed to 200 μL and incubated at 37 °C for 60 min to obtain the desired nanoprobes.

Finally, the fluorescence measurement was performed at excitation wavelength of 490 nm (λex1), emission wavelengths of 520 nm (λem1) and 578 nm (λem2). The excitation slit and emission slit were 5 nm and 10 nm, respectively.

First, 20 μL of DNAzyme was added with 100 μL of 100 μg· mL-1 MnO2 nanosheets and incubated at 37 °C for 60 min. Then, 10 μL of 1 mM GSH, different concentrations of miRNA-155, 2 μL of 40 U·μL−1 RNase inhibitor and Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 7.4) were added. The mixture was fixed to 200 μL and incubated at 37 °C for 120 min. Finally, the fluorescence measurement was performed at the conditions in 2.1.

Feasibility of cell imaging HeLa cells were divided into two groups in parallel. One group was a control with adding 200 μL of phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and 800 μL of culture medium. The second group was added with 200 μL of DNAzyme-MnO2 nanoprobes and 800 μL of culture medium. All cells were incubated at 37 °C for 6 h. All the cells were washed three times with PBS before the imaging experiments unless otherwise stated.
A laser confocal microscope was used to set the excitation wavelength at 488 nm and fluorescence images of 500–540 nm (green channel) and 560–600 nm (red channel) were collected.

Different incubation times HeLa cells were divided into five groups in parallel, 200 μL of DNAzyme-MnO2 nanoprobes and 800 μL of culture medium were added to each group, respectively. Five groups of cells were incubated at 37 °C for 1, 2, 3, 4, and 6 h, respectively.

Different types of cells DNAzyme-MnO2 nanoprobes (200 μL) and 800 μL of culture medium were added to HepG2 cells and normal cells (human umbilical vein endothelial cells, HUVEC), respectively. Then incubated at 37 °C for 6 h.

The detection principle of miRNA-155 is shown in Fig. 1 . The middle of the probe DNA3 is modified with fluorophore 6-carboxyfluorescein (FAM) and ribonucleotide (rA) respectively, while the 5′ end of the probe DNA4 is modified with fluorophore tetramethylrhodamine (TAMRA). The probe DNA3 and DNA4 can be hybridized to form a stable duplex (DNAzyme) through base complementary pairing. After the formation of the duplex, the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) occurs between the fluorophore FAM and TAMRA [49].

MnO2 nanosheet can quench the fluorescence of many fluorophores, including FAM and TAMRA. The nucleobases of the DNAzyme probes and the MnO2 nanosheets can be bound together through coordinated physical adsorption, thereby to quench the fluorescence of FAM and TAMRA. Therefore, when MnO2 nanosheets are present, the fluorescence signal of the system is very weak.

MnO2 nanosheets loaded with DNAzyme probes enter the cells smoothly through endocytosis and are reduced to Mn2+ by GSH in the cells and release the probes, resulting in the recovery of fluorescence signal of the probes. The generated Mn2+ acts as a cofactor to activate the catalytic activity of the DNAzyme probes and cleaves the probes into two fragments, further miRNA-155 can hybridize with one of the fragments to form a duplex, leading to FAM away from TAMRA, and the FRET effect is weakened. Finally, this method can achieve the purpose of detecting miRNA-155.

The morphology of MnO2 nanosheets characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) is shown in Fig. 2 A. The MnO2 nanosheets exhibit a wrinkled sheet structure. From the ultraviolet absorption spectrum (Fig. 2B), the MnO2 nanosheets have a wide absorption peak (300–600 nm), indicating that the MnO2 nanosheets have a good quenching effect on fluorescence in this range. Fig. S1A (supporting information) shows the potential analysis of MnO2 nanosheets, indicating that the MnO2 nanosheets are negatively charged as −34.22 mV. The size analysis chart (Fig. S1B) shows that the average size of the nanosheets is about 100 nm, which ensures the MnO2 nanosheets can be used as nanocarrier delivery probes to enter cells.

In order to verify the detection feasibility of miRNA-155, we controlled the experimental reaction conditions and obtained the response signals as shown in Fig. 3 . In Fig. 3A, curve a represents the fluorescence intensity of the DNAzyme probe. The results show that after the probe DNA3 hybridizes with DNA4 to form a duplex, FRET can occur, which causes the fluorescence signal of the donor (FAM) to decrease and the fluorescence signal of the acceptor (TAMRA) to increase. In addition, the presence of Mn2+ (curve b) does not affect the fluorescence intensity of the system before adding the target. Comparing curve c and curve d, the results show that only in the presence of Mn2+, the target can cause the fluorescence intensity of the system to change, which proves that Mn2+ is one of the indispensable factors in the experimental system.

In Fig. 3B, comparing curve a and curve e, the results show that the MnO2 nanosheets can quench the fluorescence intensity of the DNAzyme probe. GSH can reduce MnO2 nanosheets to Mn2+, thereby releasing the probes and restoring the fluorescence signal of the probes (curve f). After adding the miRNA-155 to the system (curve g) the generated Mn2+ as a cofactor for the DNAzyme probe can cleave the DNA strand at a specific site, and miRNA-155 can hybridize with part of the cleaved probe to form a duplex, causing FAM to stay away from TAMRA, the FRET is blocked and the fluorescence signal changed. In summary, the above results prove that this method is feasible to detect miRNA-155.

In order to get the best detection effect, the ratio of probes was optimized. As shown in Fig. S2, as the molar ratio of DNA3 to DNA4 increases, the ratio of fluorescence intensity (the ratio of the acceptor fluorescence intensity FA to the donor fluorescence intensity FD) also increases gradually. When the molar ratio is 1:8, the ratio of fluorescence intensity reaches the maximum. Therefore, the optimal molar ratio of DNA3 to DNA4 is 1:8.

In order to investigate the fluorescence quenching ability of MnO2 nanosheets, probes DNA3, DNA4 and DNAzyme were incubated with different concentrations of MnO2 nanosheets respectively to determine the fluorescence changes. The results are shown in Fig. S3. It shows that the fluorescence intensity of the probes gradually decreases with the increase of the concentration of the nanosheets, indicating that the MnO2 nanosheets can quench the fluorescence of the probes DNA3, DNA4 and DNAzyme. Subsequently, the recovery effect of GSH on the fluorescence of the probes was explored, and the results are shown in Fig. S4. GSH can restore the fluorescence of the probes, which proves that MnO2 nanosheets can be reduced by GSH to generate Mn2+, resulting in the release of the probes adsorbed on the nanosheets and the fluorescence is restored.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of variance for linear model
ce:section.ce:para: The residual diagrams of the linear model are obtained by SPSS software to verify whether there is systematic error in the analysis procedure, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . As can be seen from the residual histogram (Fig. 5A), the linear residual average value obtained by this method is 7.00 × 10−15, and the standard deviation is 0.894. The left and right sides of the figure are almost symmetrical, and the data presents a normal distribution (standard normal distribution). Fig. 5B is the P–P diagram of the standardized residuals. It can be seen that the scattered points are almost all close to the oblique line, proving that the linear regression is achieved.

The residual scatter plot (Fig. 6 ) showed that the standardized residual plot is distributed around the value of 0, which is basically symmetrically distributed up and down, and the distribution characteristics do not change with the increase of the predicted value, proving that the conditions of homogeneity of variance and independence of data meet the requirements.

In order to further prove that there is a significant linear relationship between the logarithm of the concentration of miRNA-155 and the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of the system, the analysis of variance is used (Table S2-S4).

The results of selective experiments for detecting miRNA-155 are shown in Fig. 7 . As shown in Fig. 7A, when adding miRNA-155, the ratio of the fluorescence intensity generated by the system is the highest, while when adding Mis-1, the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of the system decreases slightly. It shows that single mismatched base has great influence on target detection. When adding Mis-2, the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of the system decreases significantly, indicating that two mismatched bases have little influence on target detection, which proves that the target RNA is the only factor that causes a drastic change in the fluorescence signal of the system. When adding Mis-3 or Random, the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of the system is very low, which is almost the same as that of the blank experiment, indicating that the fluorescence response of the system only depends on the target RNA, thus proving that proposed strategy has relatively good specificity for the target miRNA-155 detection.

In order to prove that the designed probe is a DNAzyme probe that depends on the response of Mn2+, the same concentration (5 mM) of Mn2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Co2+, and Cd2+ were added to the DNAzyme probe, and then 1.0 × 10−7 M of miRNA-155 was added for fluorescence measurement. The results are shown in Fig. 7B. When Mn2+ is added, the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of the system is the highest, while when other metal ions are added, the ratio of the fluorescence intensity of the system is very low, indicating that only Mn2+ can trigger cleavage of the probe, thus proving that the probe is DNAzyme that depends on the response of Mn2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Imaging of miRNA-155 in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were used to determine the cytotoxicity of MnO2 nanosheets. As shown in Fig. S5, after incubating HeLa cells with different concentrations of MnO2 nanosheets, more than 90% of the cells still maintain high activity.

Feasibility of cell imaging In situ imaging of intracellular miRNA is of great significance for the detection and early diagnosis of cancer. As shown in Fig. S6, after incubating with the DNAzyme-MnO2 nanoprobes for 6 h, the strong green fluorescence signal (FAM) and the weak red fluorescence signal (TAMRA) can be seen in HeLa cells. However, no fluorescence signal is observed after incubating HeLa cells in the blank group with phosphate buffer solution (PBS). The results indicate that the MnO2 nanosheets can load the probes into the cells, and the intracellular GSH can reduce the MnO2 nanosheets to generate Mn2+ and release the probes. The miRNA-155 in the cells reacts with the cleaved probes, reducing the FRET effect of the probes and changing the fluorescence signal.

Incubation times The DNAzyme-MnO2 nanoprobes were incubated with HeLa cells for 1, 2, 3, 4, and 6 h, respectively. It can be clearly observed from Fig. 8 A that the green fluorescence signal (FAM) gradually increases as the incubation time increases. When the incubation time exceeds 3 h, the red fluorescence signal (TAMRA) gradually weakens, which proves that the DNA probes are consumed. Fig. 8B is the fluorescence intensity histogram after normalization of fluorescence imaging results (Fig. 8A) by using Image J software, and the change trend of fluorescence signals can be clearly seen.

Different types of cells The DNAzyme-MnO2 nanoprobes were incubated with HepG2 cells and normal cells (human umbilical vein endothelial cells, HUVEC) for 6 h, and the results of fluorescence imaging are shown in Fig. 9 . It can be clearly observed that the green fluorescence signal (FAM) in HepG2 cells is significantly stronger than that in normal cells, while the red fluorescence signal (TAMRA) is significantly weaker, indicating that the expression level of miRNA-155 in normal cells is lower than that in HepG2 cells [56]. Fig. 9B is the fluorescence intensity histogram after normalization of fluorescence imaging results (Fig. 9A) by using Image J software, and the fluorescence signals in different types of cells can be clearly seen.

Some researchers have also conducted cell imaging studies on miRNA-155, for example, Yue et al. [58] constructed a DNA machine based on branched catalytic hairpin assembly to detect miRNA-155 and studied the expression levels of miRNA-155 in different cells. Meng et al. [59] integrated DNA-encapsulated Au nanoparticles into a porous 3D hydrogel network for imaging miRNAs in living cells. Yang et al. [60] reported a responsive DNA “nano wheel” for imaging miRNA-155 in living cells. These methods all show that the expression level of miRNA-155 in cancer cells is higher than that in normal cells, which is consistent with our results (Table 2 ).
Fluorescence increased percentage represents the percentage increase in fluorescence intensity of cancer cells compared to normal cells.

In this work, we developed a new DNAzyme-based proportional fluorescent method for imaging miRNA-155 in living cells using MnO2 nanosheets as a carrier of probe and the source of Mn2+. Mn2+ is an effective cofactor to activate the catalytic activity of DNAzyme probes, thus avoiding the exogenous addition of metal ions. In addition, the proportional fluorescence imaging strategy can avoid the false results due to failed probe delivery. The proposed fluorescent method has been successfully applied to the imaging of miRNA-155 in HeLa cells and HepG2 cells, which provides great help for the diagnosis of cancer and the study of miRNA-related biology.

Yuan Zhang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Writing-Original Draft. Chunmeng Ma: Formal analysis. Chen Ma: Formal analysis. Yueci Xiang: Formal analysis. Shuai Mu: Formal analysis. Zhixiang Zheng: Formal analysis. Xiaoyan Liu: Writing-Reviewing and Editing. Haixia Zhang: Writing-Reviewing and Editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21575055) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 51862029).
